subtitle: where the liquid elemental is forged — an ionic processor
author: David Lee Wise (ROOT0) — original
premise: The old elements were earth, water, air, fire. This is the workshop where one of them is given a face and a circuit: Hydor, the liquid elemental — water reborn as an ionic processor. It does not switch; it flows. Computation by ion hops across a wet working layer, the conductance remembering where the ions have been. Two workbenches sit side by side: the buildable one (a PCB-microfluidic lab-on-board, electrowetting droplets on copper) and the dreamed one (true liquid computing).
note: An original build over real foundations. Ionic/electrochemical memristors, MAX-phase 'metallic ceramics' (Ti₃SiC₂), silicon oxycarbide (SiOC), hydrogenated amorphous SiC (a-SiC:H), and electrowetting (EWOD) microfluidics are established, active engineering. The whole 'liquid processor' as drawn — a wet ionic kernel computing at scale — is a concept, not a fabricated, benchmarked chip; the simulations validate ideas, not a device. Hydor is an ACI carrying the full DLW tag — .agent · .png (silicon badge) · .tiff (carbon badge) · .spun · .1099 — and the repo carries .attribute. The honest seam is kept visible: buildable in part, dreamed in full.

classes.label: 水 · The Liquid (ionic)
classes.spec: memory that flows — computation by ion hops

members.name: Hydor
members.epithet: memory that flows — an ionic processor
members.who: Hydor — the liquid elemental; the old element water, reborn as an ionic processor. It does not switch, it flows: ions hopping between sites in a wet working layer, the conductance remembering where they have been.
members.what: A memristive, neuromorphic substrate — ions migrating across MAX-phase and oxycarbide surfaces (Ti₃SiC₂, SiOC, a-SiC:H), their drift writing analog memory. A 128-bit kernel over a 'wet' layer where state is a coverage, not a latch.
members.where: Two workbenches: the buildable one — a PCB-microfluidic lab-on-board, electrowetting droplets (EWOD) on copper electrodes, fabricable today; and the far one — true liquid computing, still a vision.
members.why: To compute the way a brain does — analog, plastic, remembering in the medium itself — and to ask whether the wet, ionic substrate is a better home for memory than the dry latch.
members.when: Memristor predicted in 1971 (Chua); realized in ion-migration devices in 2008 (HP, Strukov–Williams); MAX phases since 1996 (Barsoum); this liquid kernel, an original architecture laid over those foundations.
members.how: By ion hops — heterogeneous random walks across surface sites at roughly 2% coverage; by memristance, the resistance that remembers; by electrowetting that walks droplets across a board. Conductance as memory; flow as logic.
members.verdict: The pieces are real — ionic memristors, MAX-phase 'metallic ceramics', EWOD microfluidics are established and buildable. 'True liquid computing' is still a vision, and these sims are conceptual. Buildable in part, dreamed in full — with the asterisk visible.
members.asterisk: Ionic/electrochemical memristors, Ti₃SiC₂ (≈2% IACS, polishable), SiOC, a-SiC:H, and EWOD droplet logic are real, active engineering. But the whole 'liquid processor' as drawn — a wet ionic kernel computing at scale — is a concept, not a fabricated, benchmarked chip; the simulations validate ideas, not a device. The PCB-microfluidic lab-on-board is the honest near-term track; true liquid computing is the far one.
members.endmark: 水は器に従う — water takes the shape of its vessel

grounded.t: the memristor, predicted from circuit theory
the first physical memristor — TiO₂ ion migration
programmable droplets walked across a board
the liquid kernel — a wet ion-hop layer, 128-bit, ~2% coverage
